title: 'We need integration so Oldham 2001 riots don't happen again'

input.lead: The man charged with bridging racial divides after the violence in 2001 calls for deeper integration
input.summary: The man charged with bridging racial divides after the violence in 2001 calls for deeper integration
input.evidence_text: On 26 May 2001, tensions between white and Asian young people in Oldham erupted in three days of violence, which later spread to Burnley and crossed the Pennines to Bradford Prof Ted Cantle was commissioned by the then Home Secretary David Blunkett to lead a review into what caused the violence in the aftermath Cantle said while some of the latent racism that existed in 2001 had disappeared, "the parallel lives that I described 25 years ago definitely still exist" "They've got a little bit better in one or two places and a little bit worse in others," he said In his report, Cantle highlighted deep-rooted segregation between ethnic groups leading what he described as "parallel lives" where those from different backgrounds did not mix He advised the government and councils at the time to ensure schools were less segregated

final_reason: Missing draft_line.

title: Manchester University work placement plans offer students 'hope'

input.lead: The university says the plans would give graduates a better chance in the job market
input.summary: The university says the plans would give graduates a better chance in the job market
input.evidence_text: Duncan Ivison, vice chancellor of the University of Manchester, has announced plans to offer students industry experience or learning partnerships during the course of their degrees He said while the plans were ambitious they had "no choice" but to do this to give students a better chance in the job market after graduation, amid increasing challenges such as Artificial Intelligence (AI) He said the plans would involve investment and building a team of colleagues to support more than 30,000 students throughout the process The initiative includes not just placements but other opportunities such as projects between local, national and global partners such as projects that involve students having to use their skills Prof Ivison said: "You've got a very difficult labour market, you've got a very volatile political situation, you've got AI transforming people's lives in all kinds of ways, tough economy "I think we need to give some hope and aspiration to young people as much as anything else."

title: Family pay tribute to boy, 17, who died Pick Mere

input.lead: David Tita Jr, 17, died after getting into the water at Pick Mere in Cheshire
input.summary: David Tita Jr, 17, died after getting into the water at Pick Mere in Cheshire
input.evidence_text: David Tita Jr, from Crewe, had been in Pick Mere, between Knutsford and Northwich, with friends on Tuesday afternoon before he was reported missing A major search was launched by emergency services at about 16:50 BST - and his body was recovered from the lake later that evening "No words will ever describe the loss of our son," David's family said in a tribute. "This pain in our chests will never leave us and will forever be within our hearts." His death came during a period of record-breaking heat across parts of the UK There have been 12 water-related deaths recently, including that of a 12-year-old boy whose body was found in the River Ribble, Ribchester, Lancashire Young people have also died in water-related incidents in Kent, Oxford, Lincoln, Halifax, Rotherham, Warwickshire, Farnborough and Scotland, along with a man in his 60s in Cornwall and a woman in her 70s in Wales

title: Pair jailed over 139mph fatal crash in 30 zone

input.lead: The pair filmed themselves inhaling nitrous oxide, running red lights and weaving through traffic
input.summary: The pair filmed themselves inhaling nitrous oxide, running red lights and weaving through traffic
input.evidence_text: Uways Hussain and Usmon Mahmood filmed themselves inhaling nitrous oxide from a balloon, running red lights and weaving through traffic before hitting 50-year-old Sylvester Abayomi, who was on his way to work Hussain was jailed for 11 years and eight months for causing death by dangerous driving, while Mahmood was jailed for 12 years and nine months for aiding and abetting causing death by dangerous driving Greater Manchester Police said the crash on Manchester's Kingsway was "one of the worst disregards of speeding" officers had seen Sentencing at the city's crown court earlier, Judge Nicholas Dean said the collision on 9 March was "wholly unnecessary and entirely avoidable" "It was a sustained, deliberate and escalating course of highly dangerous conduct over a prolonged period," he said. "It was terrifying." The victim's partner Denise Doyle described him as "the most caring soul and gentle person you could ever meet"

title: No second retrial for men accused of Manchester Airport brawl

input.lead: Muhammad Amaad and Mohammed Fahir Amaaz denied assaulting a Greater Manchester Police officer in 2024
input.summary: Muhammad Amaad and Mohammed Fahir Amaaz denied assaulting a Greater Manchester Police officer in 2024
input.evidence_text: The Crown Prosecution Service said it would not be ordering a further hearing for Muhammad Amaad, 26, and Mohammed Fahir Amaaz, 21, after two previous juries failed to reach verdicts at Liverpool Crown Court The pair had denied assaulting Greater Manchester Police constable Zachary Marsden near the car park area of Terminal 2 on 23 July 2024 Amaaz was previously convicted of assaulting two female officers, PC Lydia Ward and PC Ellie Cook, and a Starbucks customer during the same alleged incident. He will be sentenced on 26 June Amaaz appeared via videolink from custody while Amaad sat with his lawyers in the court as prosecutor Paul Greaney KC outlined the reasoning for not applying for a second retrial He said the law was clear in that there would be a "presumption and expectation" that there are no third trials save for "exceptional circumstances" Judge Neil Flewitt KC directed that verdicts of not guilty be recorded against the two defendants

title: Man who sprayed bottle of semen on women in Cheshire jailed

input.lead: William Kidd was jailed for four years in a hearing at Manchester Minshull Street Crown Court
input.summary: William Kidd was jailed for four years in a hearing at Manchester Minshull Street Crown Court
input.evidence_text: William Kidd, 27, was convicted of 25 offences and sentenced to four years imprisonment in a hearing at Manchester Minshull Street Crown Court At a previous hearing, he pleaded not guilty to 11 counts of sexual assault, eight counts of filming women underneath their clothing and one count of possession of cannabis - but he changed his pleas to guilty at the hearing on Friday He previously pleaded guilty to five counts of voyeurism The court heard Kidd, of no fixed abode, committed a number of offences against women in shops in the Macclesfield and Handforth areas of Cheshire and the Denton area of Greater Manchester from May to September 2025 He was caught on CCTV following a number of women around various shops before spraying liquid from a bottle onto them, without their knowledge. He was also recorded filming some women from behind, underneath their clothing When asked why he was spraying the liquid at women, he told one security guard, "I'm a sex addict", and another, "I did it to get a sexual thrill"

title: Whit Friday band contests take place in Tameside and Saddleworth

input.lead: A tradition dating back more than 200 years takes over towns across Greater Manchester
input.summary: A tradition dating back more than 200 years takes over towns across Greater Manchester
input.evidence_text: The Whit Friday Band Contests kicked off in Tameside and Saddleworth today, with thousands of keen brass fans arriving across the towns and up to 50 bands expected at the busiest venues The contests are open to all and competitors marked by a nearby judge, who gives a blind score for each. Awards - including best band, best youth band and best soloists - are handed out at each contest "It's an incredibly important day for the bands and community," Phil Goodwin, Delph Band conductor in Saddleworth told BBC Radio Manchester earlier "We bring together everyone and it's not just our local village - people travel now from all over the world to come and witness this," Goodwin continued "We've got children whose parents are still playing in the senior band, and we've got grandparents here too still playing "It is incredibly important to keep the tradition alive."

title: Great Manchester Run 2026 start times and road closures

input.lead: About 35,000 people are set to take part in the run with major routes closed for most of the day
input.summary: About 35,000 people are set to take part in the run with major routes closed for most of the day
input.evidence_text: Elite athletes will line up alongside fun runners for either the 10km (6.1 miles) race or the longer half-marathon course People have been urged to carefully plan their journeys into the city centre as many major roads will be closed for much of the day Both the 10k and half-marathon courses start on Portland Street in the city centre and move down into Trafford to loop around Manchester United's ground This includes a lap of the Imperial War Museum and Salford Quays before a grandstand finish at Deansgate More than 150,000 spectators are expected to line the streets in support Double Olympic champion Dame Kelly Holmes returns to the starter's platform for the elite women's race

title: Roads to close across Wigan and Bolton for Ironman competition

input.lead: Road users are being urged to plan ahead as one of Greater Manchester's biggest weekends of sport rolls into town
input.summary: Road users are being urged to plan ahead as one of Greater Manchester's biggest weekends of sport rolls into town
input.evidence_text: Temporary road closures are expected as the Ironman Bolton weekend returns from Friday 5 June to Sunday 7 June, the Local Democracy Reporting Service reports Closures for the main event on Sunday will extend from Pennington Flash to Bolton - mainly affecting Chorley New Road and the town centre. The town centre and Middlebrook retail park will be open as normal all weekend With thousands of people taking part in three different events across the weekend, Bolton Council said the temporary closures were necessary to ensure the safety of residents, competitors and spectators The weekend will kick off with a night run around Bolton town centre on the Friday and a children's event on the Saturday For the main event on Sunday, a one-loop 1.2-mile swim takes place at Pennington Flash Country Park, near Leigh, starting at 06:15 BST Following the swim, the 56-mile bike course starts at Pennington Flash, heads onto Atherleigh Way (A579), continues through Chew Moor and Lostock before returning to Bolton

title: 'Undesirable behaviour' prompts closure of Manchester walkway

input.lead: About 2,000 people use the Piccadilly Gardens cut-through, which planners have approved for closure
input.summary: About 2,000 people use the Piccadilly Gardens cut-through, which planners have approved for closure
input.evidence_text: The walkway from Piccadilly Gardens to Portland Street in Manchester passes through the ground floor of the One Piccadilly office block Following repeated incidents of antisocial behaviour and "ongoing concerns about undesirable use of the space", a plan has been put forward to close it at either end so it can only be used by people entering the building Manchester City Council's planning committee approved the scheme, with a planning officer saying they "didn't think it would have a significant impact on movements relative to the area" Two objectors claimed closing off the walkway would have a negative impact on food and drink businesses based on the building's ground floor A report to the committee said the "sheltered environment of the walkway provides opportunity for... forms of antisocial behaviour that are a continuing nuisance for occupiers" Listen to the best of BBC Radio Manchester on Sounds and follow BBC Manchester on Facebook , X , and Instagram . You can also send story ideas via Whatsapp to 0808 100 2230

title: Former supply teacher admits taking upskirt photos of pupils

input.lead: Police says it first received reports of Rahman's offending last year
input.summary: Police says it first received reports of Rahman's offending last year
input.evidence_text: Abusali Rahman, 36, who worked at schools across the north-west of England, admitted 21 offences at Bolton Crown Court Appearing via videolink from HMP Altcourse, he pleaded guilty to 12 counts of taking indecent photographs of children by upskirting, four counts of publishing an obscene article, three counts of making indecent photographs of children, one count of voyeurism and one count of dist Emma Kehoe, defending, said: "Mr Rahman knows this is a prison sentence but he was going through a mental health crisis at the time." She asked for a psychiatric report ahead of sentence Recorder Geoffrey Lowe said: "This is a complex case." Greater Manchester Police said Rahman was arrested in June 2025 after a parent raised concerns when an image of their child in school uniform was circulated on social media

input.summary: Ten members of a gang who lived lavished lifestyles including spa days funded by "peddling drugs" on the streets have been jailed Ringleader Paddy Nolan, 29, of Nelson, Lancashire, was in charge of bringing kilos of cocaine into the county for distribution by his gang, who also e
input.evidence_text: Ten members of a gang who lived lavished lifestyles including spa days funded by "peddling drugs" on the streets have been jailed Ringleader Paddy Nolan, 29, of Nelson, Lancashire, was in charge of bringing kilos of cocaine into the county for distribution by his gang, who also ensured money owed by buyers was collected Ross Boys, Nolan's most trusted mid-level marketeer, and Darren Mott, the gang's cash courier who was caught with £100,000 cash on him, were arrested alongside Nolan in 2024 The gang all pleaded at Burnley Crown Court to conspiracy to supply cocaine. Nolan was jailed for 11 years and eight months, Boys for seven years and six months and Mott for six years and nine months Evidence showed during the investigation that Nolan, of Hallam Road, had amassed a significant amount of wealth and had access to large amounts of cash, allowing him to live a lavish lifestyle, Lancashire Police said Boys, of Burnley, Lancashire, was a trusted member of the drugs network who supplied mid-level amounts of cocaine to other dealers in east Lancashire

input.evidence_text: City Centre Before it was Sacha's, there was a reason Manchester's most notorious hotel had windowless rooms Sacha's on Back Piccadilly had a whole other life before it was transformed into a hotel View 7 Images Sacha's Hotel, Back Piccadil Comments News Lee Grimsditch Features Writer 07:26, 30 May 2026 It may be one of the most infamous hotels in Manchester, but Sacha's on Back Piccadilly had a whole other life before it became what it is today. Its former use also reveals a tragic, unsolved murder and helps explai Long before it became Sacha's Hotel, the building was one of central Manchester's flagship retail destinations. Originally, it was built for C&A, an international clothing retailer C&A – or Coats and 'Ats as it was sometimes known – was the popular fashion giant founded in 1841 by German-Dutch brothers Clemens and August Brenninkmeijer. They used the initials of their first names to name the enterprise before it became a major UK retailer Clemens and August’s ancestors had long been successful traders in northern Holland, leaving the family farm in 1671 to sell their wares as travelling linen merchants. And it was in Mettingen in the 1830s that Clemens and August were apprenticed, little more than boys, to learn t By the start of the 20th century, the foundations had been laid for C&A to play a major role in making the latest fashions accessible and affordable. By 1922, it had opened its first British store, and from there the brand continued to grow, opening sites in Teesside, Hull, Liver

View 2 Images Bekka Johnso Comments News Edward Barnes Wirral Local Democracy Reporter 07:26, 30 May 2026 A Stockport café owner says overgrown weeds and ‘treacherous’ moss are leaving her part of the town centre feeling ‘forgotten’ – when £7m is being spent nearby Bekka Johnson, owner of the Covent Garden café on Lower Hillgate has slammed Stockport council over the state of a small public space next to her business. The area is dotted with a few planters, trees, and a frog which have seen better days and weeds can be seen growing out of t She told the Manchester Evening News she used to spend her own money doing up the area but with the rising cost of running a business, she could no longer afford to do that. Bekka said she has recently repeatedly tried to contact the council to raise the issue with no luck She added: “I just haven’t got the money any more. Do I either pay my ladies or do the flowers for the council? “That is where we are at with the choices with money. Everything has been squeezed so much that there isn’t spare money to do that. I keep it clean but it’s quite a lost area at the moment

Instead, it's a daily r The 39-year-old single mum Gemma, who has a two year-old daughter, was made homeless just six months ago. Originally from Shaw, the council offered her a flat in the Coldhurst area of Oldham “It’s a two bed flat but on the second floor with no lift, so getting up and down with the shopping and a little one is a challenge” she tells the Manchester Evening News as she stands holding her daughter’s hand outside a parade of shops north of the town centre “The area is actually quite quiet. I have not seen any drama in the time I’ve been here. It’s just very unclean to look at.” But, she adds, the area’s appearance is currently the least of her worries: “I work full-time, but paying nursery fees and the general cost of living, it just has a massive impact

A cordon was put in place as structural experts were drafted in And it has now been fully demolished, Trafford Council confirmed this evening (Friday). input.evidence_text: Courts Sick confessions of a pervert looking for a 'thrill' William Kidd has been jailed News Chris Slater Senior Reporter 18:26, 29 May 2026 View 2 Images William Kidd (Image: Cheshire Police) A 'predator' who violated women at shops across Greater Manchester said he 'did it for a sexual thrill'. William Kidd, 27, from Stockport, was caught four times on CCTV following women at stores before spraying a liquid at them from a bottle He aimed at their hair or backs, with his victims often unaware what had happened, Cheshire Police said. Forensic analysis later revealed the substance was Kidd's semen He struck at shops in Stockport and Denton in neighbouring Tameside - as well as Macclesfield, Handforth and Stanley Green in Cheshire - between May and September last year When confronted by a security guard he said: "I'm a sex addict." He told another: "I did it to get a sexual thrill." Police said Kidd was once seen following a woman, who was shopping with her five-year-old daughter, and filming her He was identified following a public appeal and arrested last October. A search of his phone uncovered disturbing footage, including a video following a woman pushing a child in a shopping trolley while he filmed up her skirt

title: 'Two minutes is all it takes': Parents' message following beloved son's death

input.summary: Tameside 'Two minutes is all it takes': Parents' message following beloved son's death 'One life can be lost, but up to nine lives can be saved because of that one loss' News Bethan Finighan Science and Innovation Writer 17:59, 29 May 2026 View 4 Imag The parents of a Tameside ma
input.evidence_text: Tameside 'Two minutes is all it takes': Parents' message following beloved son's death 'One life can be lost, but up to nine lives can be saved because of that one loss' News Bethan Finighan Science and Innovation Writer 17:59, 29 May 2026 View 4 Imag The parents of a Tameside man who tragically died after a seizure led to a series of cardiac arrests have issued a 'clear message' to the public Mark Thorley, an award-winning artist and JD Williams worker, suddenly died during the summer of last year, aged just 34 His heartbroken mother Dawn Letham previously told the Manchester Evening News that Mark had epilepsy, which was controlled by medication - but he suffered 'catastrophic brain damage' during a seizure that caused him to have two cardiac arrests It was on that devastating day in Tameside hospital when a member of the organ donation team informed Dawn and Mark's stepdad since he was 10, Alan, were told their son was on the organ donor list - and that their permission was needed to start the process By donating his organs, Mark saved five other lives. A man in his forties received one of Mark's kidneys and his pancreas after three years on the waiting list, while a man in his twenties received the other kidney after over four years waiting

The carnage unfolded at around 11.30am on Wednesday morning (May 27) "There were children crying, and old ladies," a witness told the Manchester Evening News . He added: "Three lads ran in... one with a gun, one with a baseball bat, and then one who waited in the hallway. The guy with the gun shouted 'don't anyone move' "There was a guy buying gold and silver who jumped up, grabbed the guy with the gun and then shouted out to his mate, who hit his head with the bat. Then I ran at them, picked up a chair and threw it at the lad. Then they ran out."

input.evidence_text: City Centre Before it was Sacha's, there was a reason Manchester's most notorious hotel had windowless rooms Sacha's on Back Piccadilly had a whole other life before it was transformed into a hotel View 7 Images Sacha's Hotel, Back Piccadil Comments News Lee Grimsditch Features Writer 07:26, 30 May 2026 It may be one of the most infamous hotels in Manchester, but Sacha's on Back Piccadilly had a whole other life before it became what it is today. Its former use also reveals a tragic, unsolved murder and helps explai Long before it became Sacha's Hotel, the building was one of central Manchester's flagship retail destinations. Originally, it was built for C&A, an international clothing retailer C&A – or Coats and 'Ats as it was sometimes known – was the popular fashion giant founded in 1841 by German-Dutch brothers Clemens and August Brenninkmeijer. They used the initials of their first names to name the enterprise before it became a major UK retailer Clemens and August’s ancestors had long been successful traders in northern Holland, leaving the family farm in 1671 to sell their wares as travelling linen merchants. And it was in Mettingen in the 1830s that Clemens and August were apprenticed, little more than boys, to learn t By the start of the 20th century, the foundations had been laid for C&A to play a major role in making the latest fashions accessible and affordable. By 1922, it had opened its first British store, and from there the brand continued to grow, opening sites in Teesside, Hull, Liver

William Kidd, 27, from Stockport, was caught four times on CCTV following women at stores before spraying a liquid at them from a bottle He aimed at their hair or backs, with his victims often unaware what had happened, Cheshire Police said. Forensic analysis later revealed the substance was Kidd's semen He struck at shops in Stockport and Denton in neighbouring Tameside - as well as Macclesfield, Handforth and Stanley Green in Cheshire - between May and September last year When confronted by a security guard he said: "I'm a sex addict." He told another: "I did it to get a sexual thrill." Police said Kidd was once seen following a woman, who was shopping with her five-year-old daughter, and filming her He was identified following a public appeal and arrested last October. The shop , run by Tommy Dolan for more than three decades, was heralded a local ‘landmark’ by those who remember its bonkers advertising and wild designs and poster The showroom has fallen into disrepair in recent years. input.evidence_text: City Centre Before it was Sacha's, there was a reason Manchester's most notorious hotel had windowless rooms Sacha's on Back Piccadilly had a whole other life before it was transformed into a hotel View 7 Images Sacha's Hotel, Back Piccadil Comments News Lee Grimsditch Features Writer 07:26, 30 May 2026 It may be one of the most infamous hotels in Manchester, but Sacha's on Back Piccadilly had a whole other life before it became what it is today. Its former use also reveals a tragic, unsolved murder and helps explai Long before it became Sacha's Hotel, the building was one of central Manchester's flagship retail destinations. Originally, it was built for C&A, an international clothing retailer C&A – or Coats and 'Ats as it was sometimes known – was the popular fashion giant founded in 1841 by German-Dutch brothers Clemens and August Brenninkmeijer. They used the initials of their first names to name the enterprise before it became a major UK retailer Clemens and August’s ancestors had long been successful traders in northern Holland, leaving the family farm in 1671 to sell their wares as travelling linen merchants. And it was in Mettingen in the 1830s that Clemens and August were apprenticed, little more than boys, to learn t By the start of the 20th century, the foundations had been laid for C&A to play a major role in making the latest fashions accessible and affordable. By 1922, it had opened its first British store, and from there the brand continued to grow, opening sites in Teesside, Hull, Liver

input.evidence_text: Tameside 'Two minutes is all it takes': Parents' message following beloved son's death 'One life can be lost, but up to nine lives can be saved because of that one loss' News Bethan Finighan Science and Innovation Writer 17:59, 29 May 2026 View 4 Imag The parents of a Tameside man who tragically died after a seizure led to a series of cardiac arrests have issued a 'clear message' to the public Mark Thorley, an award-winning artist and JD Williams worker, suddenly died during the summer of last year, aged just 34 His heartbroken mother Dawn Letham previously told the Manchester Evening News that Mark had epilepsy, which was controlled by medication - but he suffered 'catastrophic brain damage' during a seizure that caused him to have two cardiac arrests It was on that devastating day in Tameside hospital when a member of the organ donation team informed Dawn and Mark's stepdad since he was 10, Alan, were told their son was on the organ donor list - and that their permission was needed to start the process By donating his organs, Mark saved five other lives. A man in his forties received one of Mark's kidneys and his pancreas after three years on the waiting list, while a man in his twenties received the other kidney after over four years waiting

input.lead: Oldham Heart attack survivor 'didn't leave room for a year', but one thing turned his life around Terry Blinco, 47, said his heart attack three years ago sparked a 'mental breakdown' News Bethan Finighan Science and Innovation Writer 17:29, 29 May 202 A heart attack survivor from
input.summary: Oldham Heart attack survivor 'didn't leave room for a year', but one thing turned his life around Terry Blinco, 47, said his heart attack three years ago sparked a 'mental breakdown' News Bethan Finighan Science and Innovation Writer 17:29, 29 May 202 A heart attack survivor from
input.evidence_text: Oldham Heart attack survivor 'didn't leave room for a year', but one thing turned his life around Terry Blinco, 47, said his heart attack three years ago sparked a 'mental breakdown' News Bethan Finighan Science and Innovation Writer 17:29, 29 May 202 A heart attack survivor from Oldham who was so worried about his health that he "refused to leave his room for 12 months" has told of the turning point that sparked a huge four-stone weight loss Terry Blinco suffered a heart attack in August 2023, which lead to him having two stents placed and being put on medication for the rest of his life The 47 year-old said the life-changing event sparked a "mental breakdown", causing him to stay at home for a whole year "due to the fear of having another heart attack" But during this period, his health slowly deteriorated after he gained five stone. When a routine blood test showed he had become pre-diabetic, he reluctantly began a type 2 diabetes prevention course on the NHS Terry admits he wasn't looking forward to the programme, called Healthier You , and didn't enjoy the first session due to his mental health. However, he reached a turning point when his family told him not to give up

And it’s not just Reform candidate Rob Kenyon’s old tweets about Carol Vorderman On a former scrapyard in the mining village of Bickershaw sits 25,000 tonnes of stinking toxic waste. As Chris Gee reports , the ‘ocean of rubbish’ first came to light last year and later a week-long fire saw dangerous fumes released into the atmosphere and forced nearby schools A criminal investigation is underway to find out who was responsible, with a 58-year-old man currently on bail after being arrested in Birmingham. But attempts to clean-up the rubbish, which includes thousands of black and blue bin bags full of household waste like nappies, have View 5 Images It's one of the largest illegal dumps in the country And in the meantime, the dump is providing the grim backdrop to one of the most significant by-elections in recent memory

Firefighters have begun their second day at the scene on Linnett Close, in Longsight , this morning (May 29) Crews were called out to a fire involving an acetylene cylinder on an industrial unit at around 6pm last night (Thursday). Greater Manchester Police confirmed a number of properties were evacuated while crews worked at the scene Greater Manchester Fire and Rescue Service (GMFRS) said crews remained at the scene this morning 'overseeing a cooling phase' and ensuring the area is safe. A witness told the Manchester Evening News that residents were evacuated from a number of homes on nearby Lamb Close on Thu He said: "They were worried the acetylene tank was going to explode. Some people stood on the street, others went to stay with family." Images from the scene on Thursday evening show police had cordoned off an area close to the fire. An industrial yard could be seen being hosed down where the fire had broken out, with one fire engine in attendance

input.evidence_text: Courts Brothers in Manchester Airport case cleared of assaulting armed police officer as third trial ruled out Mohammed Fahir Amaaz and Muhammad Amaad have been acquitted of assaulting PC Zachary Marsden. Amaaz was previously convicted of assaulting t News Andrew Bardsley Court reporter 11:40, 29 May 2026 Updated 18:15, 29 May 2026 Two brothers have been cleared of assaulting an armed police officer at Manchester Airport after two juries failed to reach verdicts in their case Prosecutors in the case announced they would not seek a third trial after a second jury was left deadlocked last week. Mohammed Fahir Amaaz, 21, and Muhammad Amaad, 26, were both on trial accused of assaulting PC Zachary Marsden. They denied the single charge and claimed they wer Amaaz was previously convicted of assaulting two police officers and a member of the public in the same incident, trial jurors heard in the case, which ended on May 20. He now faces sentencing on June 26 for those charges Judge Neil Flewitt KC ordered not guilty verdicts to be recorded against Amaaz and Mr Amaad on the charge of assaulting PC Marsden, occasioning him actual bodily harm, after prosecutors offered no evidence Jurors in the second trial said they could not agree on any verdicts in respect of either of the defendants after deliberating for around 19 hours and 48 minutes, and after being told they could reach majority verdicts

title: 'If you carry on, you'll be dead or in prison for a very long time': Drunk arsonist given chilling warning

input.lead: Crown Court 'If you carry on, you'll be dead or in prison for a very long time': Drunk arsonist given chilling warning James Black was scalded by a judge after he torched a homeless man's tent in Manchester city centre View 2 Images James B News Charlotte Fisher Reporter 11:33, 2
input.summary: Crown Court 'If you carry on, you'll be dead or in prison for a very long time': Drunk arsonist given chilling warning James Black was scalded by a judge after he torched a homeless man's tent in Manchester city centre View 2 Images James B News Charlotte Fisher Reporter 11:33, 2
input.evidence_text: Crown Court 'If you carry on, you'll be dead or in prison for a very long time': Drunk arsonist given chilling warning James Black was scalded by a judge after he torched a homeless man's tent in Manchester city centre View 2 Images James B News Charlotte Fisher Reporter 11:33, 29 May 2026 A homeless man was rescued from his burning tent after it was torched by a drunk stranger. Gary O'Donnell was left without shelter after arsonist James Black inexplicably struck The 51-year-old was caught on CCTV walking up to Mr O'Donnell's tent on Corporation Street in Manchester city centre just after midnight on November 21 last year He was seen setting it on fire before walking away towards Market Street . Manchester Crown Court heard he had been drinking heavily as it was the anniversary of his mother's death People were seen walking past the burning tent, until somebody rushed in to rescue Mr O'Donnell. Police later arrived. Black said he had dropped a joint, but believed it had gone out A judge rubbished those claims, saying it was 'clearly entirely deliberate', adding: "Arson in this context is a form of violence."

Emergency services rushed to the scene after part of former the Tommy’s House of Fires store, in Stretford , crumbled Sections of Talbot Road and Chester Road were closed and buses diverted, but fortunately no injuries were reported. The shop , run by Tommy Dolan for more than three decades, was heralded a local ‘landmark’ by those who remember its bonkers advertising and wild designs and poster The showroom has fallen into disrepair in recent years. Pictures from the scene of the collapse show the roof was warping and bricks decaying underneath, as rubble was piled up on the ground in front. Trafford council has now said it will be using emergency powers to fully demoli View 2 Images House of Fires was open for more than 30 years (Image: Dolan Archive) This needs to be done to ‘protect public safety’. Demolition and ‘associated safety’ works got underway this morning (Friday, May 25)

But a change in conditions is expected by Met Office forecasters, with cooler temperatures and wetter weather expected in the coming days The region saw top temperatures above 30C earlier in the week, while 26C was recorded by the Met Office as Rostherne, near Altrincham , yesterday (May 28). Conditions will feel cooler from today (Friday), with a top temperature of 22C forecast in Greater Manchester Dry weather is expected throughout the day, but more cloud cover is forecast than in recent days, with a clear, sunny evening. Saturday will see the best of the weekend's weather, with highs of 25C forecast in the region and clear sunshine throughout the day But a spell of cloudier and potentially wetter conditions will begin on Sunday. Overcast conditions are forecast early on Sunday morning, with a chance of showers before lunchtime Clouds are expected to dominate in the afternoon, with sunny intervals later in the day and highs of 20C. The future of Ryebank Fields, in Chorlton , has been subject to fierce debate since it was identified as an area for housing developmen The almost five-hectare green space is owned by Manchester Metropolitan University (MMU), which used it as playing fields until 1996. However, these closed in 2020 ‘following the discovery of hazardous materials on the surface of the land’ Campaign group Friends of Ryebank Fields (FORF) hit out at potential development, and a protest camp was established in April 2021. Initially comprising tents, the camp stayed in place for more than a year and eventually included an ‘eco house’ constructed from materials ‘out of A planning application to build 120 homes on the site was submitted to Manchester City Council by housing developer Step Places, the Southway Housing Trust and MMU and last February View 3 Images The plans include 120 new apartments and townhouses (Image: Copyright Unknown)

input.evidence_text: Makerfield by-election 2026 Something stinks in Makerfield, and it's not just dirty politics There's 25,000 tonnes of stinking toxic waste bang in the middle of the by-election - and it's one of the country's biggest illegal waste dumps Comments News The former pit village of Bickershaw was once renowned for its proud coal mining heritage. But more recent notoriety for the place involves an overground accumulation of 25,000 tonnes of toxic waste rather than the rich mineral resources deep below Last summer an ‘ocean of rubbish’ emerged and later a week-long fire saw tonnes of waste burned, emitting dangerous fumes and forcing nearby schools to close A criminal investigation is underway to find out who was responsible for dumping the rubbish at the former scrapyard on Bolton House Road. Attempts to clean-up the nightmare dump were then hampered by arguments over who should pay for it Only in March did the government say it will directly fund the clean-up with the Environment Agency (EA) given more powers to enforce waste crime. But angry locals have expressed surprise at how multiple lorries carrying rubbish to the site piled waste up to 20 metres high over s Three months after the clean up was announced the dump is still there with shocking images showing thousands of black and blue bin bags full of household rubbish like nappies as well as harmful chemicals and plastics littering the area

It says 11 drivers have been left thousands of pounds out of pocket UNISON North West regional organiser Paddy Cleary said: "Trust bosses have failed to act in time and workers have been left with no choice but to strike. input.summary: Three spectacular species are neck and neck in Greater Manchester for the coveted title of Britain’s Favourite Butterfly and every vote could make the difference. Wildlife charity Butterfly Conservation is calling on the people of Greater Manchester to back their favourite butter

The The Mill investigation linked three of the four official backers of alleged ‘fake’ Independent candidates in St Peter’s – Marie Fairhurst and Muhammad Ali – to the campaign launch party of Labour candidate Atta Ul-Rasool Holding politicians to account in a way that results in real life consequences isn’t just a win for us, it’s a win for democracy in Greater Manchester . input.evidence_text: ‘Bee-spoke’ book and family sessions at Museum are due soon The bee has long been a symbol of pride and working together in Manchester and now that history has made its way into a new book dedicated to the city’s children and with a series of offers in conjunction with Manchester Museum The book, called Billie Bee and the secret glue that holds Manchester together, is a story of friendship, acceptance and adventure, with the irrepressible Billie Bee getting into a sticky situation which ultimately leads to major discovery for Manchester.....but, can the hive sur Written, illustrated and produced by the Council’s communications team, the book has been dedicated to all the city’s children for being kind, and is being made available in print, digital and audio formats, along with an activity pack, to families across the city for free The move, launched today on World Bee Day, is part of the Council’s support for families during the cost-of-living crisis and is also part of its drive to help with reading across the city, as well as a focus on how it connects to Manchester One of the book’s central themes is around kindness – also a key tenet of Manchester Museum, which aims to be the most inclusive, imaginative and caring museum that people can visit

title: St Mary's Parsonage: Public realm and movement strategy revamp for historic city centre neighbourhood

input.lead: City Council is looking to appoint a multidisciplinary team to shape a coordinated public realm and movement strategy for the St Mary’s Parsonage neighbourhood
input.summary: City Council is looking to appoint a multidisciplinary team to shape a coordinated public realm and movement strategy for the St Mary’s Parsonage neighbourhood
input.evidence_text: City Council is looking to appoint a multidisciplinary team to shape a coordinated public realm and movement strategy for the St Mary’s Parsonage neighbourhood A strategic regeneration framework (SRF) for the St Mary’s Parsonage area was agreed in August 2020 to help guide the principles for development in the area, which is bound by Bridge Street, Deansgate, The River Irwell and Blackfriars Road A substantial development pipeline is now in place in St Mary’s Parsonage, set to deliver over 1.2 million sq ft of high-quality commercial space, support over 10,000 jobs, hundreds of new homes, new hotel provision and improved, activated ground floors and public spaces This transformation is anchored by the major redevelopment of the landmark former Kendals department store building, alongside key schemes at Albert Bridge House, the Alberton and Cardinal sites, and Reedham House, as well as continued investment in heritage assets such as Arkwri To support this growth the Council is now seeking a team, to bring forward a cohesive approach to maximise the potential for the spaces between the buildings, ensuring new development creates a more attractive, accessible neighbourhood, which delivers high quality public realm an There are opportunities to activate smaller street scenes, enliven green spaces and create a real sense of place – while also enhancing the existing assets, such as Motor Square, access to the River Irwell, and Parsonage Gardens

title: Building momentum: Another record year for affordable housing in Manchester

input.lead: City Council has achieved another record year for building new affordable homes, four years into its ambitious 10-year housing strategy for the city
input.summary: City Council has achieved another record year for building new affordable homes, four years into its ambitious 10-year housing strategy for the city
input.evidence_text: City Council has achieved another record year for building new affordable homes, four years into its ambitious 10-year housing strategy for the city Almost 1,000 of the 4,766 new homes completed across the city in 2025/26 were affordable, with 439 of these 901 affordable homes available for social rent This represents the most successful year since the mid-1990s for social and affordable home building in the city but there is much more to come as momentum accelerates There are around 1,450 further council, social and genuinely affordable homes already on site now with around 2,000 more such homes ready to start, 2026/27 looks set to be another bumper year The Council set out its 10-year housing strategy in 2022 with a bold commitment to help deliver 36,000 homes across across all tenures and areas of the city with at least 10,000 of these social, council or genuinely affordable homes, and at least 3,000 of these in the city centre With 2,430 such affordable homes already completed and a strong pipeline of future projects already either under construction, with planning permission and being readied for site, or on the horizon, there is every confidence that these targets will be met or exceeded

title: The Greater Manchester Neurodevelopmental (ND) Early Help Model - Stockport Council

input.lead: You may have heard that Greater Manchester is introducing a new way of deciding which children and young people receive an NHS autism or ADHD assessment, and how quickly they are seen.
input.summary: You may have heard that Greater Manchester is introducing a new way of deciding which children and young people receive an NHS autism or ADHD assessment, and how quickly they are seen. We know this may raise questions or concerns for families In this article, we’re sharing clear
input.evidence_text: You may have heard that Greater Manchester is introducing a new way of deciding which children and young people receive an NHS autism or ADHD assessment, and how quickly they are seen. We know this may raise questions or concerns for families In this article, we’re sharing clear and simple information about what these changes mean in Stockport, and where you can get advice and support. Some local details are still being finalised, and we’ll add updates as soon as they are confirmed. For more information, you can also We understand how hard it can be waiting for an assessment, and how difficult it can be to know where to turn for help in the meantime. That’s why we’ve introduced a new way of getting early support from the moment you first notice signs that your child or young person may be aut We know this is a lot to take in. If you’d like to find out more, ask questions, or talk things through, you’re very welcome to come along to one of our information drop-in sessions The Strategic Lead for Neurodivergence in Stockport will be attending the following drop-in sessions supported by the Child and Adolescent Mental Health Service (CAMHS), Parents and Carers Together Stockport (PACTStockport), and Stockport Parents of ADHD Children in Education (SP In-person session with CAMHS and PACTStockport on Monday 11th May 10.30am-12pm, at Alma Lodge, 149 Buxton Road, Stockport, SK2 6EL. Refreshments will be provided. Please reserve a spot for this session

title: Oldham Council secures £5.7 million to support residents through financial crisis

input.lead: Oldham residents in need of financial help will get the crisis support they need thanks to a commitment agreed by Oldham Council this week for 2026/27
input.summary: Oldham residents in need of financial help will get the crisis support they need thanks to a commitment agreed by Oldham Council this week for 2026/27
input.evidence_text: Oldham residents in need of financial help will get the crisis support they need thanks to a commitment agreed by Oldham Council this week for 2026/27 The funding will deliver more than £5.7 million of support, an increase of over £1 million on the previous Household Support funding. title: Huge Medieval event to celebrate Rochdale's historic roots - Rochdale Borough Council

input.lead: A massive Medieval fair, with music, activities, combat displays and more, is set to take over Rochdale town centre this June
input.summary: A massive Medieval fair, with music, activities, combat displays and more, is set to take over Rochdale town centre this June
input.evidence_text: A massive Medieval fair, with music, activities, combat displays and more, is set to take over Rochdale town centre this June Located in Rochdale Town Hall, Town Hall Square and the newly restored Broadfield Park Slopes, Rochdale Medieval Fayre, named in a nod to the historic word for fair, will transport visitors back in time to the Medieval period where they will see history burst into colourful life Taking place over the weekend of Saturday, 6–Sunday, 7 June 2026, visitors can expect combat displays, live cookery demonstrations and reenactments of daily life from the period, which was from 1066 to around 1485 Around 80 actors from 12th century living history group, Historia Normannis, will be showcasing life as it was hundreds of years ago, including re-enactments of Medieval law courts A full Medieval encampment will be set up with Medieval fashion shows and music from the period, while visitors can try their hand at coin striking and archery sessions The event, which is being funded by the National Lottery Heritage Fund as part of the Broadfield Park Slopes restoration project, will shine a spotlight on Rochdale town centre’s rich historic roots

final_reason: Intra-batch topic duplicate — same story kept from stronger source.

title: Rochdale to benefit from sewer heat recovery to warm hospital, schools and homes - Rochdale Borough Council

input.lead: Wastewater from a sewer in Rochdale is set to supply 28GWh of low-cost, low-carbon heating to the town’s hospital, schools, leisure centre and homes
input.summary: Wastewater from a sewer in Rochdale is set to supply 28GWh of low-cost, low-carbon heating to the town’s hospital, schools, leisure centre and homes
input.evidence_text: Wastewater from a sewer in Rochdale is set to supply 28GWh of low-cost, low-carbon heating to the town’s hospital, schools, leisure centre and homes Supported by £1 million from the Government’s Green Heat Network Fund, the project will link public buildings in the town centre, with work due to start in 2029 as part of Rochdale’s ongoing town centre regeneration Heat networks are expected to play an important role in cutting heating emissions, which made up more than a third of the borough’s emissions in 2023. Whilst this technology is relatively new in the UK, wastewater heat recovery is already used at over 500 sites worldwide 2 heat sources were assessed, and a town centre sewer source heat pump was chosen as the best option, which extracts the thermal energy found in wastewater using a combination of heat exchangers and a heat pump, with an energy centre planned for the current Mecca Bingo car park n By removing the need for individual heat pumps, boilers and hot water tanks in buildings, the network would provide cheaper, cleaner heating, with lower energy bills and carbon emissions with heating and hot water delivered through a heat exchanger about the size of a small gas b Councillor Tricia Ayrton, deputy leader of the council and cabinet member for climate change and environment, said

title: Metal recycling scheme provides £17,000 summer boost for Andy's Man Club - Rochdale Borough Council

input.lead: Andy’s Man Club has been awarded a £17,000 donation by Rochdale Borough Council this May through a national scheme that recycles metals such as artificial joints and coffin fittings after cremation
input.summary: Andy’s Man Club has been awarded a £17,000 donation by Rochdale Borough Council this May through a national scheme that recycles metals such as artificial joints and coffin fittings after cremation
input.evidence_text: Andy’s Man Club has been awarded a £17,000 donation by Rochdale Borough Council this May through a national scheme that recycles metals such as artificial joints and coffin fittings after cremation Run by the Institute of Cemetery and Crematorium Management (ICCM) and OrthoMetals, the scheme has raised more than £20 million for good causes across the UK since it launched in 2006 The £17,000 donation raised from cremations held at Rochdale's crematoria has been sent to the men’s mental health charity, which first began in 2016 when 9 men got together to discuss mental health after club co–founder Luke Ambler sadly lost his brother-in-law, Andrew Roberts t Luke and Andy’s mum, Elaine, wanted to spare other families the same loss caused by mental health struggles, which inspired them to start the club The charity now runs more than 350 weekly groups across the UK, including 4 across Rochdale borough, supported by over 3,500 volunteers and attended by more than 6,500 people each week The donation is earmarked to help towards the work in supporting men and their families to get together to end the stigma surrounding men’s mental health and helps men to talk openly in a judgment-free, supportive environment, where it is ok to talk

title: Historic bridge lights restored and shining once again - Rochdale Borough Council

input.evidence_text: The historic lights on local landmark, Queen’s Park Bridge, are blazing once again, with final touches following its multi-million pound restoration now complete The bridge was recently partially rebuilt and restored by Heywood company, BDB Special Projects Limited, who used original drawings to carefully recreate the structure, so it has the same look and feel as it did when it was first unveiled in the 1930s As part of the project, specialists from Middleton company, Metcraft, were brought in to restore the beautiful gold and green lighting columns, which had once stood on the bridge. Although the lighting columns were beyond repair, Metcraft were able to salvage enough of the origin The lights were then reinstalled on the bridge and now have an electricity supply, enabling them to light up each evening from dusk to complement the existing street lights and highlight the bridge’s many heritage features The lights are energy efficient and can also be lit in different colours to honour special days in the calendar Councillor Shah Wazir, cabinet member for highways, said

title: Number of young people drinking, smoking and vaping in Bury falls to record low

input.lead: New figures released for Bury reveal that underage drinking is at an all-time low
input.summary: New figures released for Bury reveal that underage drinking is at an all-time low
input.evidence_text: Press release — 29 May 2026 09:36 Share Share this story Copy link Link copied! Download New figures released for Bury, as part of the Trading Standards North West Youth Survey, reveal that underage drinking is at an all-time low with 58% of young people (aged 14-17) abstaining from alcohol and those identifying as regular binge drinkers at a low of 6% The survey also showed a fall in the percentage of young people trying vapes, down from 46% in 2023 to 28% in 2026 The findings come from the survey which is carried out by Trading Standards North West to explore the attitudes and behaviour of 14-17 year olds in the region. The survey has been running since 2005 and the results are used to monitor illicit sales of age-restricted products with For the first time, the survey also examined cosmetic procedures among young people to reflect emerging trends and risks. While only 2% of respondents reported having undergone a cosmetic procedure - most commonly skin treatments, Botox or lip fillers - some reported receiving tr Trading Standards North West says this is an area that will continue to be monitored closely as part of wider work to protect young consumers from unsafe and illegal practices

The beret has previously been on display at the Victoria & Albert Museum, London Working in conjunction with the 'Victoria Wood Literary Estate', and her family members, Bury Art Museum is delighted to share original scripts, song sheets and photos all once belonging to Victoria, brought together in this exhibition to remember and celebrate her life The exhibition will also include a video montage, which shows behind the scenes footage of the filming of 'Victoria Wood's All Day Breakfast', which was broadcast on BBC One on Christmas Day 1992, and a recording of a rehearsal of ' Dinnerladies ' before a live audience

title: Health column: Smoke Free Generation - A rare landmark moment for public health

input.lead: It may have become a little lost amid major political and global events but there was a landmark moment for public health last month, the significance of which should not be overlooked On 29th April the government’s Tobacco and Vapes Bill became law, making it illegal to sell tob
input.summary: It may have become a little lost amid major political and global events but there was a landmark moment for public health last month, the significance of which should not be overlooked On 29th April the government’s Tobacco and Vapes Bill became law, making it illegal to sell tob
input.evidence_text: It may have become a little lost amid major political and global events but there was a landmark moment for public health last month, the significance of which should not be overlooked On 29th April the government’s Tobacco and Vapes Bill became law, making it illegal to sell tobacco to anyone born on, or after, 1st January 2009 - and, in doing so, creating the UK’s first ‘smoke‑free generation’ Make no mistake that this is one of the most significant public health reforms in a generation, and one that sends a clear signal: preventing harm before it begins matters Smoking remains the leading preventable cause of illness and early death in the UK. It significantly increases a person’s risk of cancer, heart disease and stroke, places huge pressure on the NHS, and continues to widen the gap in life expectancy across our communities The new law takes a bold, long‑term approach to breaking that cycle by ensuring today’s children never legally start smoking at all We know that information, advice and individual willpower can only go so far. Real, lasting change happens when policy and environments support people to make healthier choices

final_reason: Intra-batch topic duplicate — same story kept from stronger source.

title: Safer knife replacement scheme rolls out across Wigan Borough

input.lead: A move towards rounded‑tip knives is underway across Wigan Borough to enhance community safety Through its Community Safety Partnership, Wigan Council is among the first local authority to take part in the safer knife replacement scheme, a key part of the Let’s Be Blunt campaign
input.summary: A move towards rounded‑tip knives is underway across Wigan Borough to enhance community safety Through its Community Safety Partnership, Wigan Council is among the first local authority to take part in the safer knife replacement scheme, a key part of the Let’s Be Blunt campaign
input.evidence_text: A move towards rounded‑tip knives is underway across Wigan Borough to enhance community safety Through its Community Safety Partnership, Wigan Council is among the first local authority to take part in the safer knife replacement scheme, a key part of the Let’s Be Blunt campaign - set up by Leanne Lucas, a survivor of the Southport attack Nearly 20 council and partner buildings have already made the switch to the knives which feature a rounded-ended tip, as well as many residential homes taking up the pledge and making the swap Leanne Lucas said: “Replacing pointed tip knives is a simple but important step in reducing risk and preventing harm “Rounded tips make a real difference; the pointed tip creates the pressure point and increases the potential for serious injury “We also must recognise that harm is often about access and opportunity. When everyday items are easily available, they can be used in moments of crisis, impulse or vulnerability, so making small changes like this helps reduce that immediate risk

She likes to link these stories to her own experiences, such as fostering refugees and Accessibility is at the heart of everything Sally creates. title: Einstein on the Beach

input.lead: English National Opera and Factory International presents A powerful new production of Einstein on the Beach arrives at Aviva Studios in 2027 Einstein on the Beach is a new experiential production of Philip Glass and Robert Wilson’s iconic 1976 opera, created by English National
input.summary: English National Opera and Factory International presents A powerful new production of Einstein on the Beach arrives at Aviva Studios in 2027 Einstein on the Beach is a new experiential production of Philip Glass and Robert Wilson’s iconic 1976 opera, created by English National

title: Marina Abramović: Balkan Erotic Epic | Aviva Studios

input.lead: Experience the raw, unfiltered power of Balkan folklore in a new immersive show from the world’s most famous performance artist Balkan Erotic Epic is a bold new performance work created by the iconic artist Marina Abramović .
input.summary: Experience the raw, unfiltered power of Balkan folklore in a new immersive show from the world’s most famous performance artist Balkan Erotic Epic is a bold new performance work created by the iconic artist Marina Abramović . Regarded as the world’s most influential performance a
input.evidence_text: Experience the raw, unfiltered power of Balkan folklore in a new immersive show from the world’s most famous performance artist Balkan Erotic Epic is a bold new performance work created by the iconic artist Marina Abramović . Regarded as the world’s most influential performance artist, Abramović’s transformative work has reached millions of people – from opening the Pyramid Stage at Glastonbury Festival t A four-hour ritual where ancient myth meets performance art, Balkan Erotic Epic explores the eroticism, spirituality and traditions of Abramović’s homeland through 13 visceral scenes Brought to life by a cast of over 70 performers, dancers, musicians and singers, the experience unfolds across Aviva Studios. Audiences are free to choose their own path – with pop-up encounters punctuating the action, bringing bodies together in dance, song and ritual Watch Fertility Rite , a fevered ritual where bodies writhe against the ground in a desperate call for fertility. Witness Massaging the Breast where women gesticulate over graves to awaken the earth. In Scaring the Gods , women bare themselves to the sky to banish storms. Listen Unafraid to push the boundaries of eroticism and performance art, Balkan Erotic Epic asks its audience a timeless question: What are our bodies truly for?

input.evidence_text: Join this creative workshop for some stitching, chatting, and sharing inspired by the museum’s collections What happens when the workforce is fragmented and hard to organise? In this workshop we will be exploring the experiences of workers who have struggled to unionise or gain union recognition from their employers. From cleaners, hotel workers and the catering industry through to De We will learn about barriers to unionisation and the significant actions and strikes that have attempted to overcome these problems to create fairer pay and working conditions We will create textile responses to the discussions, which will be used as part of a collective textile piece in future workshops Part of PHM’s programme of activity exploring the history of strikes and solidarity If you require British Sign Language (BSL) interpretation please contact access@phm.org.uk or 0161 838 9190

input.evidence_text: No Art Open Air at Bolesworth Castle Bolesworth Castle, Tattenhall, Chester CH3 9HQ Sat 30th May, 2026 12pm - 11pm No Art is heading out of the city and into the grounds of Bolesworth Castle – not far from Chester – for what’s set to be the party brand’s biggest UK open-air show to date Taking place on Saturday 30 May, the one-day festival has been curated by Dutch duo ANOTR and marks the first time a No Art open-air event outside the Netherlands has expanded into a full two-stage format. That means a bigger site, more room for large-scale production and install ANOTR will headline the day themselves, joined by Marco Carola, Max Dean, Toman, Benja and Silvie Loto on the main stage, all artists who fit neatly into No Art’s polished, forward-thinking house world. Over on the second stage, things lean deeper and looser, with Chaos In The CB Set across 6,000 acres, the Cheshire castle site gives the event a lot more room to breathe than your standard field set-up. Organisers are promising upgraded sound, ambitious production and immersive art installations, with the countryside location also helping deliver a cleaner No Art has built its name by pairing house music with striking settings and a strong visual identity, with previous editions landing in Amsterdam, Barcelona, Miami, Rio de Janeiro and São Paulo. This one looks like its most ambitious move in the UK so far, and easily its biggest

title: Open Fire Dining Club: The Royal Oak x Tartuffe - Manchester's Finest

input.lead: Open Fire Dining Club: The Royal Oak x Tartuffe Torr Vale Mill, Torrvale Rd, High Peak, SK22 3HS Sat 30th May, 2026 - Sun 31st May, 2026 4pm - 9:30pm The Royal Oak was the name that kept coming up on a recent trip to Cartmel.
input.summary: Open Fire Dining Club: The Royal Oak x Tartuffe Torr Vale Mill, Torrvale Rd, High Peak, SK22 3HS Sat 30th May, 2026 - Sun 31st May, 2026 4pm - 9:30pm The Royal Oak was the name that kept coming up on a recent trip to Cartmel. So we went
input.evidence_text: Open Fire Dining Club: The Royal Oak x Tartuffe Torr Vale Mill, Torrvale Rd, High Peak, SK22 3HS Sat 30th May, 2026 - Sun 31st May, 2026 4pm - 9:30pm The Royal Oak was the name that kept coming up on a recent trip to Cartmel. So we went. And it more than lived up to the chatter Run by Demi Millard and Tom Pemberton, it’s a proper village pub with a very clear sense of place – rooted in the produce, landscape and flavours of the Lake District, but with cooking that goes far beyond what you might expect from a country inn. It felt like a natural fit for O On Saturday 30 and Sunday 31 May , The Royal Oak will head to Torr Vale Mill to collaborate with Tartuffe on an eight-course menu built around the Lakes , and the ingredients that define that part of the world The menu opens with Carlingford oysters, grilled over the Big Green Egg with wild garlic and crème fraîche, followed by a smoked chicken wing with Morecambe Bay brown shrimp – flavours that will immediately place you in the South Lakes At the centre of the evening is Hebridean lamb à la ficelle , served with white beans, anchoïade, courgette, dandelion, bitter leaf salad and The Royal Oak’s mint sauce. It’s exactly the kind of generous, elemental dish Open Fire Dining Club was made for

input.evidence_text: Yan Tan Tethera Farmer’s Market at Track Track Brewing Co - Brewery & Taproom, Unit 18, Piccadilly Trading Estate, Manchester M1 2NP Sun 31st May, 2026 12pm - 8pm Yan Tan Tethera Farmer’s Market is back for its May edition, and this time it’s turning the dial up a bit On Sunday 31 May, the market returns as a full Food Festival edition, bringing together farmers, growers, chefs, drinks, music and a very strong line-up of Northern kitchens for a one-day celebration of the start of summer Already confirmed are Cafe San Juan, Erst, Freds, Stockport Provisions, Track Beer and Slice Culture Pizza, with fresh oysters, street food and more names still to be announced In proper Yan Tan Tethera style, each food vendor will be paired with one of the market’s produce stalls to create special one-off dishes built around what’s in season. Think chefs and growers side by side, swapping ingredients, cooking over fire, and leaning into a bit of creati The market takes its name from the old Northern shepherd’s counting system, a nod to rural life, seasonal rhythms and the people who work the land. He and his band the Night Sweats are based in Denver, Colorado. After fronting two earlier bands, Ratel Rateliff had begun writing songs that required a band, so he formed the Night Sweats. Their rowdy retro sound was deeply influenced by vintage soul, R&B and gospel traditions, Van Morrison and the Band. A formidable live unit with a reputation for stage-quaking shows, they signed Nathaniel Rateliff & the Night Sweats’ self-titled debut album was issued in August 2015, preceded by appearances at the Newport Folk Festival and The Tonight Show Starring Jimmy Fallon. title: RHS Garden Bridgewater | Visit Salford

input.lead: The £35 million project set within 154 acres has transformed the former site of Worsley New Hall, restoring the historic walled garden, lake and surviving buildings Originally built for the 1st Earl of Ellesmere between 1840 and 1845, Worsley New Hall was a grand Gothic-style man
input.summary: The £35 million project set within 154 acres has transformed the former site of Worsley New Hall, restoring the historic walled garden, lake and surviving buildings Originally built for the 1st Earl of Ellesmere between 1840 and 1845, Worsley New Hall was a grand Gothic-style man
input.evidence_text: The £35 million project set within 154 acres has transformed the former site of Worsley New Hall, restoring the historic walled garden, lake and surviving buildings Originally built for the 1st Earl of Ellesmere between 1840 and 1845, Worsley New Hall was a grand Gothic-style mansion, complete with glorious, formal landscaped gardens It’s now one of UK’s must-see gardens with so much to enjoy for visitors of all ages Step inside the magnificent 11-acre Weston Walled Garden, the site’s jewel in the crown. One of the largest Victorian walled gardens in the UK, it showcases a range of growing techniques and edible plants There’s also the spectacular Paradise Garden showcasing exotic planting inspired by Asiatic and Mediterranean gardens. The orchard and nearby bee and butterfly garden, have been designed to attract wildlife The Peel Learning Garden, supported by The Peel Group, will help schoolchildren learn how plants affect our lives and the planet, whilst the Community Wellbeing Garden provides a space for people to garden, socialise and relax

Attenda The forum is being convened by Manchester Digital , Cooperative Network Infrastructure , Greater Manchester Combined Authority and LINX , reflecting a shared commitment to strengthening the UK’s digital foundations and ensuring the benefits of growth are felt across the country The UK is entering a critical phase in the development of its digital infrastructure. This guided walking tour will bring those stories to life. Led by a local expert, the experience is set to be a lively introduction to the people, places and ideas that have shaped one of the UK’s most influential cities The tour will begin at Manchester Central Library, that impossible to miss, grand circular landmark on St Peter’s Square. The neoclassical design has made it one of the city’s most-loved buildings, holding thousands of stories in its walls and on its shelves. The square itself ha From here, the tour takes you through the city’s heart, down the colourful streets of the Gay Village and the calming Sackville Gardens. It is here that you will see the monuments that honour Manchester’s role in the fight for equality and the city’s pioneering LGBTQ+ community a This guided tour is filled with monuments and tales from many moons ago. The Medieval Quarter is the perfect place to soak in all that history. It is here where you will see the oldest public library in the English-speaking world, Chetham’s Library . Housed in a beautiful sandsto During all this walking, your guide will point out local street art, hidden corners and stories you’d easily miss on your own. Tales of suffragettes, reformers, musicians and everyday Mancunians who’ve all left their mark on the city in their own way

Not always the first place people associate with colour, design and typography – but Tom Hicks has spent years looking closely enough to challenge that. In Black Country Type II at The Modernist, the photographer turns his attention once again to this post-indu The exhibition marks the publication of the second volume in Hicks’ ongoing Black Country Type project. The first volume sold out quickly after its 2023 release and went on to attract international attention. Following strong demand for more of Hicks’ work, this new instalment br Again, the photographs come from Hicks’ freeform walks and cycle rides through his native region, eying up details that might otherwise evade attention. His interests intersect in each shot: art, typography, architecture, design, popular culture and history. Some capture hand-pai In this project, Hicks works with direct sunlight – a risky dependency for a British photographer – using it to heighten colour and contrast. Altrincham’s charter dates back to 1290, it’s location at the intersection of the Cheshire farm belt and the commercial commuter zone of Manchester means the town has a long and important history. title: Manchester City

input.lead: The best images from City's latest session ahead of the FA Cup final...
input.summary: The best images from City's latest session ahead of the FA Cup final... The Blues travel to Wembley on Sunday 31 May to take on Brighton & Hove Albion Kick-off is set for 15:00 (UK), with Andrée Jeglertz’s side looking to complete a domestic double with a win over the Seagulls Ci

The Blues travel to Wembley on Sunday 31 May to take on Brighton & Hove Albion Kick-off is set for 15:00 (UK), with Andrée Jeglertz’s side looking to complete a domestic double with a win over the Seagulls City claimed the WSL title with a game to spare earlier this month, before booking our place in the FA Cup final just four days later in dramatic fashion Two goals down heading into the final five minutes of the 90, late goals from Mary Fowler and Khadija ‘Bunny’ Shaw forced extra time against reigning champions Chelsea Shaw then sealed a memorable comeback triumph in stoppage time, heading home to send City to our first final since 2022 Standing in our way, though, are a Brighton side who ended the WSL campaign in excellent form, getting the better of City in our league encounter last month
